**ACCEPT**
- [ ] words with more than 1 char, possibly containing numbers (e.g. "word", "w0rd").
- [ ] words having the character ' literally (e.g. "O'Reilly", "didn't")
- [ ] numbers (e.g. "1", "2", "2017")

**REFUSE**
- [ ] words with a single char which is not a number (e.g. "A", "i", "x")

offline explained, symbolic dim is used,
1, if there's one symbolic dim and all others are 1, then symbolic dim will be used in result shape.
2. if there're symbolic dims and one dim value > 1, then the result shape will take the >1 value.
3. if there're more than 1 symbolic dim and all others are 1, then the result shape will not be set.

With the old `target_link_libraries(mxnet mxnet_static)`, doing `target_link_libraries(my_target mxnet)` will link both `mxnet` and `mxnet_static` into `my_target`. That is how cmake treats PUBLIC and INTERFACE link libraries of a target. By marking it PRIVATE this is no longer the case. You can still link `mxnet_static` explicitly, but it will not be added simply by linking `mxnet`.

For more information see also:
https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.0/command/target_link_libraries.html
https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.0/prop_tgt/INTERFACE_LINK_LIBRARIES.html#prop_tgt:INTERFACE_LINK_LIBRARIES

On the other hand, if you think it feels weird, other people probably also think it feels weird.

Good question, @NHDaly! I'm gonna give explaining it a shot and let me know if I am unclear. It all boils down to scoping. In JavaScript, the `this` keyword is used to refer to the object that the function you are currently in is tied to. In this case, `this` references `NotebookList`. You can confirm this by adding a `console.log(this)` statement to the beginning of the `move_selected` function. Now, let's say that inside your `move_selected` function, you create a new object with functions as you do with the dialog in this case. Your `this` now references a completely different object within those functions. You can test this out by adding a `console.log(this)` right before line 741. But what if in this new context you want to reference the `NotebookList` as you do on line 741? Well, you store the value of `this` in a variable called `that` when `this` references the object you later want to reference and then use `that` in functions where `this` does not reference that object.

So in reality, you don't particularly need it for the first few lines at the top since `this` references `NotebookList` in those cases but I like to declare at the top and use it consistently so that it is obvious that throughout the code anywhere `that` is used we are referencing a `NotebookList` object.

Let me know if any of that needs clarification! `this` is definitely a tricky topic in JavaScript.

A job can have multiple search_terms defined, but the bug filer is only sent the search_terms for a single failure line. 

That single search_terms is an array with zero or more items contained within it, but the only time one of the omitted leads should be present is when a good search_term could be found, so we just use the failure line as a whole (capped at 100 characters, I believe). 

The only time multiple items should be within search_terms is if we actually found valid things (multiple filenames, crash signatures, etc).

Don't get lost in qualifying “new functionality” or not. The algorithm is downward, not upward:

- Is there a single possible existing call to the public API that could behave differently than was expected before the change? If yes, it's a major bump. (here, based on @fpagnoux remark, it did)
- Is there a new possible call to the public API that was not expected before? If yes, it's a minor bump.
- Is there a call to the public API that was expected to work but in actuality did not, and now does when applying the changeset? Philosophically, it's a bug fix, but in terms of SemVer it's a patch bump.
- Is there some internal refactor that doesn't change anything? You may release a patch bump. But I would recommend you simply release a _pre-release patch bump_, and delay release until you actually have something to your users.

Remember: the only use of SemVer is presenting metadata on the public API and easing dependency management. Here is a [cheat sheet](https://speakerdeck.com/mattisg/git-session-2-strategies).

RGB images would be processed channel by channel. In most applications cases, this is quite fine. 
Some color space use linear combination of the RGB channels for lightness/brightness/value channel. If you convert into this space, process this channel, and convert it back, than you get approximately the same result.

Secondly, there is no perfect color space. I would not use automatic conversion inside the filters. Or if this is the decision, then make it universal everywhere in skimage. 
Maybe a decorator/wrapper could handle the conversions back and forth. 

But I think this is something what the user should do. (Does any of the function do such automatic conversion before/after processing?)

For instance, I have an RGB image, the filter converts it to HSV, process the V channel, and then?
Should it convert it back to RGB, leave it in HSV, or process it further to something else?

What about different multichannel images? E.g. 2 or 4 channels (RGBA)? If I have to choose, I would rather go for single channel version only than a multichannel with built-in color conversion, but this is just me. I would argue for consistency, and I don't see this conversion as a consistent solution across skimage. (And still: if this is the idea, then let's go for a wrapper, and do not implement it for every filter individually.)

    def make_post_request(self, url, payload, params=None, files_attached=False):
        """
            Make a POST request using the provided ``url`` and ``payload``.
            The ``payload`` must be a dict that contains the request values.
            The payload dict may contain file handles (in which case the files_attached
            flag must be set to true).
    
            If the ``params`` are not provided, use ``default_params`` class field.
            If params are provided and the provided dict does not have ``key`` key,
            the default ``self.key`` value will be included in what's passed to
            the server via the request.
    
            :return: The decoded response.
            """
        if params is not None and params.get('key', False) is False:
            params['key'] = self.key
        else:
            params = self.default_params
    
        # Compute data, headers, params arguments for request.post,
        # leveraging the requests-toolbelt library if any files have
        # been attached.
        if files_attached:
            payload.update(params)
            payload = MultipartEncoder(fields=payload)
            headers = self.json_headers.copy()

You are right about documentation. Any help will be appreciated, even to track the project only and to review and early or constructive comments. My language skills are problematic when I described a complex "moving target" and other people can not understand it in README clearly. They respond too late or propose a fix by removing. Not too motivating. I started then to write more info to Wiki and even more info to [hynekcer sf wiki](https://github.com/hynekcer/django-salesforce/wiki/), where all original project topics are linked together and some independent topics (urls) are added. (My repository looks messy but the Wiki is a text for other people or a concept of this) I can write code or documentation, but usually it is very innefficient to try to do it synchronized. The objective is to write a pseudo-documentation during development, use it for discussion and later to be rewritten to documentation by other person. I have no ambition to start multi-page documentation, even that it would be useful to differentiate e.g. easy install :-) from the reference.
 
It even would not help me to have a documentation for writing custom db backend, because nobody reasonable from Django team would promise any interface for NoSQL databases. In the recent Django versions since 1.8 you can see a backend specific code not only in `django/db/backends/', but also in 'django/db/models/', e.g. many `as_mysql` methods. There is no clean way to write an additional backend without tricky code. I remember previous versions of MongoDB for Django, where it was even more tricky due to Mongo driver for patched Django, up to version 1.5.

"Tricky"? As little as possible. Example: Your code is by the standard recommended style, e.g. the try/except branch for new/old code. My experience is, when I must observe undocumented information and also must regularly remove obsoleted code after previous Django versions, that the explicit style ("if django version > something; else;) is better for tests, debugging and also to get more useful tracebacks in issues.

Migrations: First: Search the string "migrat" in README (4 lines in 3 paragraphs) and marginally also [Introspection and Special Attributes...](https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/wiki/Introspection-and-Special-Attributes-of-Fields) (1 paragraph) ... (after you read) ...
Migrations are more and more important in Django, even the current "fake migrations" for Salesforce, if you for some reason want to use SalesforceModel with another database, e.g fast in memory tests, data copying etc. (see issues 56, 97, 98. 111, 167 that it is important.) This has been supported for a long time until Django 1.10 and fixed just now. My personal style to frequently run inspectdb on the current SFDC and run a filter to select important/unimportant fields for the final model. If SFDC should alternate with another db in development or maintenance tasks, then run makemigrations an migrate to get the same model in that database. If you can try it and explain it human way, do it please. :-)

Regarding exactly what is happening:
`next()` searches for the first entry in the `commands` list that
* is a string (because re.match will die if we hand it a non-string type)
* has the regex match for `r"(\s)?!!"`

If none are found, `next()` returns None instead of raising `StopIteration`, breaking out of the while loop
get_password() would return an empty string, and later code tests for `None`, so yes, the `or None` (or some other, more intuitive construction) is necessary.
